coredata.dc:title: Colorful, time-dependent carbon dot-based afterglow with ultralong lifetime
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Siqian
Wang, Bowei
Zan, Yuxuan
Shen, Zhuoyao
Liu, Shuai
Fang, Wangwang
Yan, Xilong
Li, Yang
Chen, Ligong
coredata.dc:description: Metal-free afterglow materials have brought about widespread attention due to application advantages. Nevertheless, it is an attractive but formidable challenge to achieve long-persistent afterglow materials with high quantum yield (QY) at ambient conditions. Particularly, those with tunable afterglow are very rare. Herein, an economical strategy is proposed to construct a series of multi-color (cyan, green and orange) afterglow materials via one-step melting (within 5–10 min) of boric acid (BA) with unconventional precursors. The highly rigid framework, B − C bond and hydrogen bond interactions can efficiently inhibit non-radiative deactivation by locking the triplet excitons in carbon dots (CDs) clusters. 66.13% high QY and 1.74 s long lifetime have been achieved simultaneously, as well as the afterglow can be observed by naked eyes for ∼ 26 s. According to the adjustment of the precursor structure, dynamic afterglow color-changing (from orange, yellow, white to cyan) material was successfully realized. These excellent features allow them to be more applicable in time-resolved information security. In brief, this work provides a simple, flexible and effective strategy to construct highly efficient afterglow materials, and further facilitates their optical regulation and various potential applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multicolor
Ultralong lifetime
Metal-free afterglow
Boric acid

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectra, time-resolved (delayed) photoluminescence spectra (Excitation wavelength: 365 nm, delayed time: 10 ms), and (b) Phosphorescence decay curves of 7HOCAx%/BA (x = 0.01, 0.25 and 1.00) composites monitored at 495 nm, 494 nm, 494 nm, respectively. (c) Photographs of 7HOCA0.01%/BA under sunlight, before and after switching off UV lamp (365 nm). Excitation power: 3 W, exposure time: 2 s.

(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of 7HOCA0.01%/BA composite. (Inset: the size distribution of CDs). (c) FT-IR spectra of BA, 7HOCAx/BA (x = 0.01%, 0.25% and 1.00%) composites. (d) PXRD spectra of 7HOCA0.01%/BA. (e) XPS survey scan and (f) C 1 s HRXPS spectra of 7HOCA0.01%/BA composite. B 1 s HR XPS spectra of (g) 7HOCA0.01%/BA, (h) 7HOCA0.25%/BA, and (i) 7HOCA1.00%/BA composites.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectrum and delayed emission spectrum of (a) DBM0.01%/BA, (b) 3ClPHB0.01%/BA and (c) NA0.01%/BA composites, respectively. Excitation wavelength: 385 nm, 365 nm, and 365 nm, respectively. Delayed time: 10 ms. Phosphorescence decay curves of (d) DBM0.01%/BA, (e) 3ClPHB0.01%/BA and (f) NA0.01%/BA composites monitored at 512 nm, 495 nm, 560 nm, respectively. (g) Digital images of the DBM0.01%/BA, 3ClPHB0.01%/BA and NA0.01%/BA composites under UV light on/off, respectively. Excitation power: 3 W, exposure time: 2 s.

(a) Schematic diagram of the possible RTP emission process for CDs/BA composites. (b) RTP mechanism for CDs in matrix by multi-confinement forces and electronic effect. (c) Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectras of boric acid (BA) and 7HOCAx%/BA (x = 0.01, 0.25 and 1.00) composites.

(a) Time-resolved (excitation wavelength: 365 nm, delayed time td: 0.01 to 8 s) photoluminescence spectra and (b) corresponding phosphorescent decay curves of (NA-CA)0.01%/BA composite at different emission peaks monitored at 388 nm, 428 nm, 418 nm, 340 nm, and 355 nm, respectively. (c) Afterglow photographs of (NA-CA)0.01%/BA composite taken after turning off the irradiation (365 nm) under ambient conditions.

Digital photos of the characters and time-resolved security features of (a) QR code made from 7HOCA0.01%/BA composite. (b) “UNOPENED” made from DBM0.01%/BA composites and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (2-AA) under daylight, before and after turning off UV lamp (365 nm). (c) Photographs and time-resolved security features of “Three Fish Play Lotus” graphic security made from 7HOCA0.01%/BA, DBM0.01%/BA, and NA0.01%/BA composites.
Schematic for the preparation of multicolor CD-based composites.

head.ce:title: Colorful, time-dependent carbon dot-based afterglow with ultralong lifetime

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaoxing Xingxin New Materials Co., Ltd., Shaoxing 312300, Zhejiang, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-free afterglow materials have brought about widespread attention due to application advantages. Nevertheless, it is an attractive but formidable challenge to achieve long-persistent afterglow materials with high quantum yield (QY) at ambient conditions. Particularly, those with tunable afterglow are very rare. Herein, an economical strategy is proposed to construct a series of multi-color (cyan, green and orange) afterglow materials via one-step melting (within 5–10 min) of boric acid (BA) with unconventional precursors. The highly rigid framework, B − C bond and hydrogen bond interactions can efficiently inhibit non-radiative deactivation by locking the triplet excitons in carbon dots (CDs) clusters. 66.13% high QY and 1.74 s long lifetime have been achieved simultaneously, as well as the afterglow can be observed by naked eyes for ∼ 26 s. According to the adjustment of the precursor structure, dynamic afterglow color-changing (from orange, yellow, white to cyan) material was successfully realized. These excellent features allow them to be more applicable in time-resolved information security. In brief, this work provides a simple, flexible and effective strategy to construct highly efficient afterglow materials, and further facilitates their optical regulation and various potential applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, afterglow materials have promising application prospects in numerous fields, including optoelectronic [1–3], displays and lighting [4,5], safety encryption [6–12], and bio-imaging [13–15], etc. However, achieving long-persistent afterglow materials is a considerable challenge owing to the intrinsically spin-forbidden transition between singlet and triplet, and fast non-radiative decay of the triplet excitons [16]. To date, efficient long afterglow materials are mainly obtained from rare earth metal complexes [17,18]. Undoubtedly, these materials have some inherent drawbacks, such as harsh preparation conditions, single afterglow color, high cost and cytotoxicity. Recently, significant progress in organic room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials has been achieved by engineered crystals [19–21], introduction of hetero atoms or heavy atoms [22–24], H-aggregation [25,26], and host–guest [27]. However, most of the reported materials still suffer from relatively short lifetime, sensitivity to oxygen, low QY, instability, and poor processability, which undoubtedly restrict their further practical applications [6,19,28]. Hence, developing a more convenient, efficient and flexible strategy to fabricate metal-free afterglow materials with simultaneous high emission efficiency and long lifetime is still extremely urgent.

As a new emerging zero-dimensional nano-luminescent carbonmaterial, CDs have aroused tremendous interest owing to its modification, low toxicity, biocompatibility, high stability, and fascinating optical performance [29,30]. However, little attention has been paid to their phosphorescent phenomenon due to inefficient intersystem crossing (ISC) rate and fast non-radiative deactivations [31,32]. Previous studies have shown that rigid matrixes can effectively stabilize triplet excitons of CDs by inhibiting molecular motion and isolating water/oxygen [33]. Recently, CD-based afterglow materials with sub-second lifetime were achieved by embedding CDs into inorganic salts [34], zeolite [35], silica [36] and polyvinyl alcohol [37] matrixes. However, they also suffer from the following disadvantages: (1) RTP efficiency and lifetime still need to be further improved, and afterglow of more than 20 s at ambient conditionshas rarely been reported; (2) most cannot effectively modulate phosphorescent properties, resulting in a single lifetime and emission color; and (3) the preparation process is considerable harsh, time-consuming and poor repeatability, as well as their scale of production is often limited. Undoubtedly, these issues significantly hinder their further development. To our best knowledge, no time-dependent afterglow colors (TDACs) with long lifetime (τ greater than 1 s) has been reported so far, though it has considerable value in the field of advanced dynamic information encryption. It is worth noting that most precursors for preparing CDs are derived from organic acid or organic amine derivatives [38–40], etc. according to the current methods. In fact, different precursors or synthetic approaches can generate CDs with various structures, and/or surface states, as well as luminescence properties [26,41]. Therefore, developing nontraditional precursors is expected to realize diversified CD-based afterglow materials. In addition, CD-based afterglow composites are usually prepared by at least two steps, in which CDs are first synthesized and then mixed into host matrix. It is not conducive to the close bonding between CDs and matrix, resulting in poor stability and low phosphorescent efficiency of the composites. However, one-step in-situ preparation of CD-based afterglow composites remain a daunting challenge due to uncontrolled formation of CDs and matrix [38,42,43].

Herein, a series of multicolor long afterglow CD-based materials were in-situ prepared, and the phosphorescent lifetime and QY were up to 1.74 s and 66.13%, respectively, via simple one-step melting treatment of BA with various unconventional organic precursors (Scheme 1 ). In the process, the triplet excitons of CDs were stabilized effectively by multi-confinement effects. The introduction of B atom with empty orbit played a critical role in boosting effective ISC process of CDs. The flexible modulation of phosphorescent behavior could be easily realized by simply adjusting precursor structure. Intriguingly, the as-fabricated (NA-CA)0.01%/BA composite exhibited fascinating TDACs property by reasonably constructing CDs with multiple phosphorescent emission centers. Furthermore, these composites were further applied in advanced information security and anti-counterfeiting based on the advantages of high stability, repeatability, and large-scale manufacturing.

All chemical reagents used were purchased from commercial source without further purification. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), High-resolution TEM images, and STEM-elemental mapping analyses were operated on a JEOL JEM-F200 electron microscope which was carried out at an electron beam voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were recorded by using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (K-Alpha + ) with an Al Kα X-ray source. The binding energy was calibrated by the C1s peak at 284.8 eV as the reference. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were carried out on Bruker AXS D8 X-ray diffractometer (Germany) using a Cu Kα X-ray source (40 kV, 100 mA). An Evolution 300 UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used for Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis). The NMR spectra were recorded by Bruker-Advanced III (500 MHz) spectrometers and TMS as the internal standards. High resolution mass spectra (HRMS) were characterized on a Bruker Daltonics micrOTOF-QII instrument. Fourier transform infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) were measured on a Nicolet 380 FT-IR spectrometer. Absolute quantum yield (QY), fluorescence, phosphorescence and afterglow decay profiles were performed on an Edinburg FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were measured on Bruker EMXnano spectrometer. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using Gaussian 09 software package at B3LYP/6-31G(d).

Firstly, 10 g of boric acid (BA) was fully mixed with 0.01% mol equiv of 7-hydroxycoumarin (7HOCA), dibenzoylmethane (DBM), 3-chloro-4-hydroxy-benzaldehyde (3ClPHB), 1,8-naphthalimide (NA), respectively. Then the mixtures were transferred into glass bottles and heated to melt in an oven at 220℃ for 5–10 min. Finally, the obtained composites were ground into powder for use. They were named 7HOCA0.01%/BA, DBM0.01%/BA, 3ClPHB0.01%/BA, and NA0.01%/BA, respectively.

7-Hydroxycoumarin (7HOCA, 2.00 mmol), excess of 1,2-dibromopropane (10.00 mmol), and K2CO3 (3 equiv.) were mixed in methanol (15 mL) and stirred for 2.5 h at 60 ℃. The reaction was monitored through thin layer chromatography. When the reaction was completed, the reaction mixture was filtered, and methanol was removed under vacuum. The crude intermediate 1 was useddirectlytothenextreaction after methanol washing. Next, intermediate 1 (1.00 mmol), 1,8-naphthalimide (1.10 mmol), and K2CO3 (3.00 mmol) were mixed in methanol (10 mL) and stirred for 5 h at 60 ℃. The reaction was monitored by utilizing TLC. When the reaction was completed, the resulting mixture was filtered, and methanol was evaporated under vacuum. The obtained residues were purified by utilizing silica gel chromatography with EA/PE (10:1) as eluent to yield target compounds NA-CA (yield: 82%). Its structure was confirmed by 1H NMR, 13C NMR spectroscopy and HRMS spectrometry (Scheme S1 and Figure S1 − S3). 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ = 8.64 (dd, J = 7.3, 1.1, 2H), 8.24 (dd, J = 8.3, 1.0, 2H), 7.78 (dd, J = 8.2, 7.3, 2H), 7.60 (d, J = 9.5, 1H), 7.37 – 7.31 (m, 1H), 6.88 (dd, J = 7.6, 2.0, 2H), 6.23 (d, J = 9.5, 1H), 4.67 (t, J = 6.3, 2H), 4.39 (t, J = 6.3, 2H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, CDCl3) δ = 164.27, 161.75, 161.19, 155.81, 143.33, 134.29, 131.67, 131.55, 128.74, 128.27, 127.04, 122.39, 113.25, 112.87, 101.82, 65.13, 38.77. HR-MS (ESI): m/z calcd for C23H15NO5Na+: 408.0848 [M + Na] +, found: 408.0820.

7HOCA0.01%/BA composite was prepared by one-step melting reaction of BA (10 g) and 7-hydroxycoumarin (7HOCA, 2.62 mg) under 220 ℃ for 5–10 min in a beaker. Excitedly, the composite powder exhibited bright blue emission under irradiation with a 365 nm UV light, and then a bright cyan long-afterglow lasting for ∼ 26 s can be observed by naked eyes after turning off the excitation source (Fig. 1 c).

Firstly, the optical properties of 7HOCA0.01%/BA composite were investigated. As shown in Figure S4a, three absorption peaks were observed in UV–vis absorption spectra of 7HOCA0.01%/BA methanol solution. They were derived from the π-π* transition from C = C (∼220 and ∼ 242 nm) and n-π* (∼324 nm) transition from C = O, respectively. Compared to 7HOCA, the emergence of a new absorption peak at ∼ 242 nm indicated the generation of new species. The fluorescence spectra of the composite in different solvents were measured. Its maximum photoluminescence peaks appeared at ∼ 390 and ∼ 456 nm under 300 nm excitation (Figure S4b). However, no phosphorescence was observed due to fast non-radiative relaxation, suggesting that the matrix can effectively activate and stabilize the afterglow performance. As shown in Figure 1a, 7HOCA0.01%/BA composite exhibited a major cyan delayed emission peak at 500 nm in time-resolved (delayed) photoluminescence spectra, showing a 120 nm red-shift compared to its steady-state (prompt) photoluminescence spectra (λ em = 380 nm). The blue color and cyan luminescence belonged to the fluorescence and RTP emission, respectively, which could be defined by the striking difference between their emission lifetimes (3.64 ns and 1.74 s, Fig. 1b and Figure S5). Moreover, its temperature-dependent delayed spectra exhibited a gradual decrease in the intensity as the temperature rose from 77 K to 300 K (Figure S6a). This is the character of phosphorescence as thermal energy can accelerate the dissipation of triplet excitons. In addition, the phosphorescent intensity had a good linear relationship with temperature, displaying the potential to be utilized as a luminescent temperature sensor. Interestingly, it also exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence and phosphorescence emission behavior (Figure S8, S9a and S10a). This could be attributed to the wide particle size distribution of CDs (0.5–2.2 nm; Fig. 2 a, inset), leading to the existence of multiple emission centers [44]. The Commission International del’ Eclairage (CIE) coordinates were calculated to demonstrate the color-tunable phosphorescent emission of 7HOCA0.01%/BA composite under different λ ex (Figure S10a). Upon excitation transferring from 285 nm to 445 nm, the phosphorescent color changed from cyan to yellow-orange. Under optimal excitation, the maximum RTP lifetime and QY were measured to be 1.74 s and 66.13%, respectively (Fig. 1b and Table S1). In addition, a clearly visible afterglow duration time of greater than 20 s from the composite was observed under the ambient conditions. As far as we know, it is one of the best RTP performance among metal-free afterglow materials under ambient conditions heretofore.

Furthermore, the effects of precursor content on phosphorescent emission were investigated. For instance, when doping content increased from 0.01% to 1.00% (in molar ratio), the RTP color of 7HOCAx/BA composites remained basically unchanged, but phosphorescent efficiency and lifetime decreased obviously (Fig. 1a and b, Table S1). When the doping content was 0.25%, the phosphorescent lifetime and QY were 1.67 s and 49.21%, respectively. When the doping content increased to 1.00%, the phosphorescent lifetime and QY were 1.23 s and 26.32%, respectively. Therefore, it can be preliminarily inferred that precursor in excess of a specified amount is not conducive to generate phosphorescent emitters.

To explore RTP emission source, the morphological and structure of 7HOCA0.01%/BA composite were investigated by utilizing high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and Raman spectrometry. High-angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HADDF-STEM) and TEM mapping images demonstrated the uniform distribution of C, O and B atoms in the composite (Figure S11). As shown in Fig. 2a and 2b, CDs clusters successfully generated in-situ and were embedded into the framework of the hybrid. The well-resolved lattice fringe spacing of CDs was 0.212 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite [45]. Meanwhile, the size distribution of CDs was mainly within the range of 0.5–2.2 nm, and the mean diameter was ∼ 1.11 nm (Inset in Fig. 2a). Furthermore, it was found that high precursor content could induce the aggregation of molecules, further hindering the in-situ generation of CDs (Figure S12). This may be the main reason for RTP performance degradation. To demonstrate the interference effects of residual precursor molecules, a bi-component system of 7HOCA and BA (1:10000 in molarratio) was constructed by solvent evaporation method. The obtained solid only showed a maximum fluorescence emission at 415 nm and undetectable RTP (Figure S13). HRTEM images showed that no CDs formed in the matrix, which further confirmed that phosphorescent emission was derived from CDs generated during the melting process (Figure S14). Furthermore, the rigid matrix could firmly inhibit the motion of CDs and shield CDs from the water and oxygen in air, greatly boosting the radiative decay of the excited triplet excitons, consequently leading to bright RTP emission.

To further understand the RTP behavior, chemical compositions of 7HOCAx/BA were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2c, the band at 3228 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration absorption of O − H. The bands located at 1456 cm−1 and 1195 cm−1 were ascribed to B − O and B − O − H bands, respectively. And the new characteristic peak at 950 cm−1 belonged to B − C band [46]. Furthermore, the PXRD pattern presented the typical peaks of B2O3 at 14.5°, 28.0°, 30.6° and 40.2° owning to dehydration of BA (Fig. 2d). The above results indicated that both B2O3 and BA matrixes existed in the system. In this case, the B − C covalent bond, rigid network structure of the matrixes and hydrogen-bonding interactions between BA and CDs could lock and stabilize the triplet excited states of CDs via suppressing their motion more effectively. XPS measurements agreed well with the FT-IR analysis. As shown in Fig. 2e, the full XPS spectrum of 7HOCA0.01%/BA showed the presence of O (48.64%), B (39.45%) and C (11.91%), and their characteristic peaks were at 283.2, 531.5, and 192.7 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2f and 2 g, the HR C 1 s band exhibited three components of C − C/C = C (284.4 eV), C − O (285.6 eV), and C = O (288.8 eV). The HR B 1 s band showed three peaks at 192.78 eV, 193.62 eV and 194.46 eV, which were assigned to BCO2, B2O3 and B − O, respectively [47]. Therefore, it can be determined that the electron-withdrawing B atoms were incorporated into CDs clusters by covalent bonds. With the increase of 7HOCA doping amount, the content of BCO2 gradually decreases (Fig. 2g − 2i), indicating the corresponding decrease in the number of B − C bonds is obviously correlated with the decrease of phosphorescent properties. As a conclusion, the special RTP behavior might be caused by the coupling of the matrix and CDs. B atom played a critical role in regulating the phosphorescence of CDs/BA composite. The empty orbit on it attracts π electrons of CDs, which can effectively reduce the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level of the system, thereby narrowing the energy gaps (ΔE ST) [48]. As a result, the ΔE ST was calculated to be 0.478 eV (Table S3). This is a small value for effective ISC process.

Inspired by the ultralong lifetime of the above CD-based composite, and to further verify the universality of the strategy, other precursors, such as dibenzoylmethane (DBM), 3-chloro-4-hydroxybenzaldehyde (3ClPHB) and 1,8-naphthalimide (NA) were doped into BA matrix to fabricate DBM0.01%/BA, 3ClPHB0.01%/BA and NA0.01%/BA composites under the same conditions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 g, all composites could emit intensive fluorescence and phosphorescence. Their fluorescence emission peaks were located at 425 nm, 494 nm and 403 nm, respectively. Impressively, different precursor endowed the products with colorful phosphorescence coverage from 494 to 560/608/662 nm (Fig. 3a − 3c). Their average lifetime could be adjusted in the range of 0.30 to 0.94 s and the corresponding QYs were 23.65%, 56.41% and 31.66%, respectively (Fig. 3d − 3f, Table S1). Temperature-dependent delayed emission spectra verified their RTP properties (Figure S6a). With the increase of temperature, their delayed spectra exhibited a gradual decrease in the intensity. This is the character of phosphorescence as thermal energy can accelerate the dissipation of triplet excitons. In addition, their phosphorescent intensity showed a good linear relationship with temperature, displaying the potential utilization of luminescent temperature sensors. For 3ClPHB0.01%/BA, both the prompt and delayed emission were centered at 440 nm shown in the steady-state spectrum (Fig. 3b and Figure S16c), indicating it was a pure RTP material. In addition, the phosphorescent spectra of these composites excited under different wavelengths were measured (Figure S9 and S10). Similar with 7HOCA0.01%/BA composite, all composites displayed excitation-dependent RTP emission at room temperature. XPS analysis demonstrated that all the composites formed B − C bonds (Figure S15). More importantly, the ΔE ST were calculated by comparing the fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra of composites at 77 K (Figure S16) and listed in Table S3, all of results are minimized values (0.477–0.882 eV) for effective ISC process.

Based on the above analysis results, a possible mechanism for achieving such afterglow materials with ultra-long lifetime and high quantum efficiency could be proposed. The CDs generated in situ and were embedded into the 3D network of BA matrix through multi-confinement forces. Firstly, the rigid network could isolate CDs from external quenchers (water and oxygen), resulting in enhanced phosphorescent properties (Fig. 4 b). Secondly, the stable B–C bonds and abundant hydrogen bond interactions between the rigid matrix and CDs could effectively stabilize the triplet excitons of CDs via suppressing the intramolecular vibration. As shown in Fig. 4a, the energy of the triplet state was greatly reduced by introducing electron-withdrawing B atoms. Subsequently, the triplet excitons were stabilized by multi-confinement forces, resulting in a more stable triplet T*. This may be the reason for the huge difference between the fluorescence and phosphorescence. To further prove that the prepared composites could promote the phosphorescent emission, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of BA and 7HOCAx%/BA (x = 0.01, 0.25 and 1.00) were recorded to characterize the stable triplet states, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4c, compared with BA, the CD-based composite exhibited higher intensity in the EPR spectras, indicating that more triplet excitons generated. Meanwhile, the intensity decreased obviously with the increase of doping content of the precursor. Therefore, it can be concluded that the precursor content was too high, less luminescence centers were left for aggregation-induced quenching effect, resulting in relatively poor phosphorescence performance. As a reference matrix, polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) was selected to assess the activation effect of the BA matrix. The 7HOCA0.01%/PMMA composite prepared under the same conditions exhibited no detectable phosphoresce at ambient conditions. The control experiments demonstrated that both electronic effect and multi-confinement forces of BA were crucial to achieve CD-based RTP. Furthermore, different precursors could effectively modulate the surface structure of CDs, resulting in tunable RTP properties.

Developing RTP materials with time-dependent afterglow colors (TDACs) is of considerable significance in the field of advanced dynamic information encryption. However, this kind of material is still extremely scarce [49-51] due to lacking reasonable theoretical guidance and effective design strategy. Hence, on the basis of the above study on relationship between CDs structure and phosphorescent properties, a novel precursor NA-CA was synthesized by linking NA and 7HOCA with an alkyl chain, and then (NA-CA)0.01%/BA composite was prepared under the same conditions. Interestingly, it exhibited obvious TDACs, turning from orange (toff = 0 s) to yellow (toff = 1 s), then to white (toff = 4 s), and finally to cyan (toff = 6 s), respectively (Fig. 5 c). To acquire deeper insights, its afterglow spectra at different delay times (td) were measured. As shown in Fig. 5a, three emission peaks appeared in the short-wavelength (478 nm) region and the long-wavelength (560/606 nm) region, and the corresponding lifetimes were 1.597 s, 0.609 s and 0.576 s, respectively (Fig. 5b). With prolonged time, the relative intensity of emission peaks in the long and short wavelength regions evolved gradually (Fig. 5a). The CIE diagrams clearly showed the colour evolution at different td (Figure S16), which was consistent with the naked-eyes observation. Obviously, (NA-CA)0.01%/BA successfully inherited the luminescence properties of the two precursors, which indicated that the surface states of the CDs possess multiple phosphorescent emission centers with difference lifetimes. Compared with the current hydrothermal synthesis method of CDs, which requires a very long time polymerization and carbonization process, this method is more suitable for regulating the surface states or structures of CDs due to short reaction time. It was worth noting that (NA + CA)0.01%/BA prepared by simple mixing equal amounts of NA and 7HOCA only showed cyan afterglow. Phosphorescent spectra showed a major emission peak at 487 nm (τ = 1.68 s), while only very weak acromion peaks appeared in the long-wavelength region (Figure S17). This further indicated that the doping of two components was not conducive to the control of the surface structure of CDs due to the randomness of CDs formation process. Thus, the strategy is promising to adjust and enrich TDACs materials by reasonable molecular design.

For phosphor materials, optical stability is considerable important in practical applications. To our delight, all the composites disaplayed extremely high stability. After being exposed to air for more than half one year or grinding, the lifetimes of all the composites were not obviously decreased (Figure S19 and S20). Furthermore, 7HOCA0.01%/BA powder maintained excellent RTP emission when dispersed in organic solvents (Figure S21). Such a high stability comes from the host matrix, which can not only efficiently shield the CDs from water/oxygen outside but also lock emissive species and restrain their intramolecular motions by B − C covalent bonds, hydrogen bonds interactions and efficient spatial restriction. Moreover, all materials have high photostability. After UV irradiation (more than 8 h), their luminescence properties almost unchanged (Figure S22).

Application. Considering the excellent RTP properties and stability, the composites showed great potential for advanced anti-counterfeiting, information security, and other applications. Fig. 6 a showed a unique encrypted QR code made with 7HOCA0.01%/BA on blue fluorescent background. They were invisible in daylight. Under 365 nm UV irradiation, the whole pattern showing bright blue fluorescence signals. After turning off the lamp, cyan QR code, composed of 7HOCA0.01%/BA, became the only visible information. Similarly, the bright blue characters “UNOPENED” written by blue-emitted 2-AA and DBM0.01%/BA could be clearly seen upon UV irradiation. After turning off the lamp, only the composite showing bright green character “UOP” could be recognized (Fig. 6b).

Based on the tunable phosphorescent properties of products, a quadruple encoding model was fabricated. In Fig. 6c, upon 365 nm lamp, a “Three Fish Playing Lotus” anti-counterfeit label drawn by CDs/BA composites displayed bright blue photoluminescence, as the first-level anti-counterfeit pattern (Fig. 6c-1). Once the excitation was turned off, the picture immediately changed to a tricolor mode (cyan, green and orange), as the second-level anti-counterfeit (Fig. 6c-2). Meanwhile, owing to diverse phosphorescence lifetime of CDs/BA composites, the pattern also evolved with time. The relatively shortest lifetime of NA0.01%/BA resulted in orange emission disappearing firstly. Subsequently, a cyan and green RTP pattern could still be identified by naked eyes, as the third-level anti-counterfeit (Fig. 6c-3). Finally, the green RTP emission of DBM0.01%/BA became invisible and only a cyan RTP pattern could still be identified by naked eyes, as the fourth-level anti-counterfeit (Fig. 6c-4). Therefore, quadruple lifetime-encoding for information encryption was realized. The whole process has the advantages of obvious boundary between multiple modes and long duration of each mode, showing great application potential in anti-counterfeiting encryption.

In summary, we have successfully proposed a general and innovative strategy by one-step melting treatment of BA with various organic molecules to prepare CD-based afterglow materials. In particular, 7HOCA0.01%/BA composite exhibits high QY (66.13%) and long lifetime (1.74 s), as well as the afterglow can be observed by naked eyes for ∼ 26 s, which is one of the best metal-free RTP materials so far. The unique phosphorescent properties benefited from the fact that the highly efficient spatial restriction, B − C covalent bond and hydrogen bond interactions efficiently inhibit non-radiative deactivation of the triplet excited states in CDs clusters. What is more, the phosphorescent color and lifetime of composites can be regulated efficiently by changing the precursor structure. Intriguingly, (NA-CA)0.01%/BA composite shows distinct multicolor (orange, yellow, white and cyan) TDACs owing to the existence of multiple phosphorescent emission centers with distinguishable lifetimes. They were further applied into advanced anti-counterfeiting and time-resolved information security. The rational combination of BA matrix and various suitable precursors would facilitate the design of many novel high-performance afterglow materials. This discovery will undoubtedly promote fundamental understanding of CD-based materials and broaden their practical applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Project funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21808161 and 21978208) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2020 T130465).
